message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 2 (34 todo)
Recent changes: Green made quote return items.length * 100 + PROCESSING_FEE.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 2 tests pass, 34 todo. Now I'll work through the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Let me evaluate every identifier in the implementation.

**Naming Evaluation**:

- `quote` — Function purpose: "given a list of items, returns the insurance premium (base premiums + processing fee)". Is `quote` clear? In the insurance domain, a "quote" is exactly the price offered for a policy. Both passing tests describe `quote({ items })` returning a premium. The name accurately reveals intent. Keep.
- `QuoteInput` — Reveals it is the input shape for `quote`. Clear. Keep.
- `input` — generic but accurate parameter name for the quote input. Acceptable. Keep.
- `PROCESSING_FEE` — Tests literally say "processing fee only" / "+ 5 fee". Name matches the domain term exactly. Keep.
- `basePremiums` — Tests say "100 base", "60 base", etc. The term "base premium" is the domain term. The plural reflects that it is the sum of per-item base premiums. Accurate and reveals intent. Keep.
- `claim` — A separate stub function for the claim tests (all still todo). Domain term "claim" is correct. Keep (untouched this phase).

Decision: All names already reveal intent clearly and use the domain vocabulary from the spec. No rename needed.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

I'll measure the `quote` function (the code changed in Green; `claim` is an untouched stub).

```
export function quote(input: QuoteInput): number {
  const basePremiums = input.items.length * 100;
  return basePremiums + PROCESSING_FEE;
}
```

Component Count:
- Constants: 2 (`100`, and `PROCESSING_FEE`'s reference is a binding) → `100` = 1
- Bindings: `input`, `input.items` (member access ~ binding), `basePremiums`, `PROCESSING_FEE` reference, `.length` → count the scalars `input`, `length`, `basePremiums`, `PROCESSING_FEE` = 4
- Invocations: 2 (`*`, `+`) = 4
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Loops: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 (the `const` is a binding, not a mutation) = 0

Total Mass ≈ 1 + 4 + 4 = **9**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules (priority order)

**Rule 1 — Tests Pass**: All 2 tests pass. Confirmed above.

**Rule 2 — Reveals Intent**: Names are clear (Step 1). One small consideration: `basePremiums` is computed inline as `input.items.length * 100`. The intermediate variable already serves as an explaining variable, separating "the base premiums" from "adding the fee". This reads well and matches the test descriptions ("100 base + 5 fee"). No improvement available without harming clarity.

**Rule 3 — No Duplication**: No duplication in `quote`. The `100` magic number could conceptually be a named constant, but there is currently only one item type and one rate; extracting `BASE_PREMIUM_PER_ITEM` would be speculative (the upcoming todo tests show per-type rates of 60/80/40/25, meaning `100` is really the sword/default rate, not a universal constant). Naming it now would risk an inaccurate name and is premature abstraction (violates Rule 4 / YAGNI). No safe extraction.

**Rule 4 — Fewest Elements**: The function is minimal — one binding, one return. The intermediate `basePremiums` binding adds one element but earns its place by revealing intent (Rule 2 trumps Rule 4). Could inline to `return input.items.length * 100 + PROCESSING_FEE;` which drops one binding (mass ~8), but that sacrifices the explaining variable that maps directly to the "base + fee" structure in the tests. Per the rule that Rule 2 trumps APP, I keep the explaining variable.

## Step 4 & 5: Refactoring Attempt + Mass

I evaluated each rule and the candidate change (inlining `basePremiums`) would lower mass by 1 but reduce clarity. Per project rules, Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP mass, so I do not apply it.

The mandatory refactoring attempt was made: I assessed naming, duplication, and element count and identified one concrete candidate (inlining the explaining variable). It was correctly rejected because it trades clarity for marginal mass reduction. There is no improvement that does not harm Rule 2.

Let me confirm tests still green (no code was changed, but verifying discipline).

content.text:
## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Evaluation**:
- Naming: `quote`, `QuoteInput`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `basePremiums`, `claim` all use the domain vocabulary from the spec and clearly reveal intent. No rename.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The `basePremiums` intermediate variable already acts as an explaining variable mapping to the test phrasing "100 base + 5 fee". Already clear.
- Rule 3 (Duplication): None present. Extracting `100` into a named constant would be premature — upcoming todo tests reveal per-type rates (60/80/40/25), so `100` is the sword/default rate, not a universal one. Naming it now risks an inaccurate name.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): The one candidate found was inlining `basePremiums` (`return input.items.length * 100 + PROCESSING_FEE;`), which lowers APP mass from 9 to ~8 but removes the explaining variable.

Reasoning: The single concrete refactoring candidate (inlining the explaining variable) was rejected because Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP mass per project standards. All other avenues (renaming, deduplication, extracting the magic number) are either unavailable or constitute premature abstraction given the current two passing tests. The current implementation is already clean and minimal for the behavior under test.

No code change applied — the existing implementation is optimal for the current test set.

## Step 7: Report Completion

```
🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
Refactoring: none applied — one candidate (inline `basePremiums`) evaluated and
             rejected because Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps the ~1-point APP
             mass reduction it would yield.
Mass Change: 9 → 9 (unchanged; clarity preserved over lower mass)
Tests: All passing ✅ (2 passed | 34 todo)

Proceeding to the next test.
```

Relevant files:
- Implementation: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- Tests: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

The next test to activate (Red phase) is: `"returns 65 G for a single plain amulet (60 base + 5 fee)"` — this will force the implementation to move beyond the flat `100`-per-item rate toward per-item-type base premiums.
